title: Figures and captions

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Figures and captions; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Figures and captions
sources.claim: Figure groups self-contained media or code with an optional figcaption that names or explains that content.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Figure groups self-contained media or code with an optional figcaption that names or explains that content.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A code screenshot and its explanatory caption are wrapped in figure while the image still has suitable alt text. Your goal is to create a semantically grouped figure and caption without using figure as a generic wrapper. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Relative and absolute URLs; Paragraph and inline meaning; Images and alternative text. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Figure groups self-contained media or code with an optional figcaption that names or explains that content. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Not every image needs figure, and figcaption does not replace required image alternatives when the image itself conveys information. This draft therefore rejects: Using figcaption means the nested image can omit its alt attribute.

includes: Associate self-contained media or examples with meaningful captions when appropriate.
Create a semantically grouped figure and caption without using figure as a generic wrapper.
excludes: Not every image needs figure, and figcaption does not replace required image alternatives when the image itself conveys information.

outcomes: Create a semantically grouped figure and caption without using figure as a generic wrapper.

examples.title: Figures and captions: concrete case
examples.situation: A code screenshot and its explanatory caption are wrapped in figure while the image still has suitable alt text.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A code screenshot and its explanatory caption are wrapped in figure while the image still has suitable alt text.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use figure for self-contained referenced content, but still provide the nested media's required alternative text.
examples.result: The result follows because Use figure for self-contained referenced content, but still provide the nested media's required alternative text.

examples.title: Figures and captions: boundary case
examples.situation: Not every image needs figure, and figcaption does not replace required image alternatives when the image itself conveys information.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Not every image needs figure, and figcaption does not replace required image alternatives when the image itself conveys information.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Using figcaption means the nested image can omit its alt attribute.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use figure for self-contained referenced content, but still provide the nested media's required alternative text.
